elements.sent-2411-001: Physics Informed Machine Learning has emerged as a popular approach in modelling and simulation for digital twins to generate accurate models of processes and behaviours of real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, despite their success in generating accurate and reliable models, the existing methods either use simple regularizations in loss functions to offer limited physics integration or are too specific in architectural definitions to be generalized to a wide variety of physical systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper presents a generic approach based on a novel physics-encoded residual neural network architecture to combine data-driven and physics-based analytical models to address these limitations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our method combines physics blocks as mathematical operators from physics-based models with learning blocks comprising feed-forward layers.
elements.sent-2411-005: Intermediate residual blocks are incorporated for stable gradient flow as they train on physical system observation data.
elements.sent-2411-006: This way, the model learns to comply with the geometric and kinematic aspects of the physical system.
elements.sent-2411-007: Compared to conventional neural network-based methods, our method improves generalizability with substantially low data requirements and model complexity in terms of parameters, especially in scenarios where prior physics knowledge is either elementary or incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-008: We investigate our approach in two application domains.
elements.sent-2411-009: The first is a basic robotic motion model using Euler Lagrangian equations of motion as physics prior.
elements.sent-2411-010: The second application is a complex scenario of a steering model for a self-driving vehicle in a simulation.
elements.sent-2411-011: In both applications, our method outperforms both conventional neural network based approaches as-well as state-of-the-art Physics Informed Machine Learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: Digital twins are virtual counterparts of any real-world system or entity synchronized with the system or entity through real-time data.
elements.sent-2411-013: A central concept in digital twins is the modelling and simulation of processes and behaviour associated with the real-world entity.
elements.sent-2411-014: These models allow users to investigate behaviour in the alternative `what-if' scenarios and can be divided into two fundamental categories: physics-based models and data-driven models.
elements.sent-2411-015: While physics-based models offer better accuracy and reliability of predictions, they are often computationally expensive and inflexible to be used in near-real-time simulations - especially in the context of Digital Twins \cite{b20, b23 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, data-driven models provide flexible and adaptive solutions, especially with a limited understanding of underlying physics.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, they popularly suffer in terms of reliability and interpretation.. \cite{b21 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The benefits of combining physics with data-driven models to target these limitations are found in several studies, especially in the context of developing simulation models in situations with limited availability of prior physics knowledge and real-world data \cite{b21, b22 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Modelling of physical systems can be categorized into three regimes based on the availability trade-off between data and known physics, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:physics_v_data .}
elements.sent-2411-020: As data increases, the balance shifts from leveraging prior physics knowledge to data-driven extraction of physical relationships.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the ubiquitous middle ground, some physics and data are known, with potentially missing parameters and initial/boundary conditions, and this is the most common scenario in models for digital twins in general.. \cite{b7 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Physics-informed machine learning is a recent domain that offers a streamlined approach to incorporate both data and the fundamental laws of physics as prior knowledge, even when dealing with models with incomplete physical information.
elements.sent-2411-023: This integration, or `bias', is achieved by regularizing the loss function of the learning algorithms to adhere to the constraints defined by the respective physics models and equations.
elements.sent-2411-024: More specialized approaches involve specifically designed neural networks, which ensure that the predictions generated adhere to the governing physical principles.
elements.sent-2411-025: One of these approaches recently gaining traction in the research community is known as `differentiable physics'.
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach allows differentiable physics models to be directly baked into neural network architectures.
elements.sent-2411-027: An example is the recent work on new connections between neural network architectures and viscosity solutions to Hamilton–Jacobi PDEs \cite{b24 as shown in Figure \ref{fig:hj_pde_pinn .}}

Here, $f$ represents the solution of the Hamilton–Jacobi partial differential equations, while $x$ and $t$ correspond to the spatial and temporal variables, respectively. The activation function $L$ is convex and Lipschitz. The parameters $a_i \in \mathbb{R}$ and $u_i \in \mathbb{R^n}$ are specific to the neural network. Image courtesy of J. Darbon and T. Meng, Brown University and cited here from \cite{b7}.} \label{fig:hj_pde_pinn}
elements.sent-2411-028: While existing work mainly focuses on isolated physical phenomena \cite{b29, b30, b31 , and lacks the generalization of the approaches to wider problems, we propose Physics Encoded Residual Neural Network (PERNN), a general framework to capture complex behaviour of real-world systems.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our approach aims to target scenarios where data availability from the target systems is limited to specific operating scenarios and known physics only captures specific regions of the behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our approach defines a hybrid neural network which comprises feature extraction blocks to compute unknown variables (learning blocks) and combines them with differentiable physics operator layers (physics blocks) to model the complete behaviour of the target system.
elements.sent-2411-031: To facilitate the convergence of the model, we introduce intermediate `residual' layers between the physics and the learning blocks.
elements.sent-2411-032: We also make our code available publicly on GitHub.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/saadzia10/Physics-Encoded-ResNet.git In this way, domain knowledge can inform the underlying neural network architecture while the gradient descent optimization using data can fill in gaps \cite{b32, b33 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The goal is to leverage interpretable mechanistic modelling to improve generalization and avoid relying solely on empirical correlations \cite{b34, b35 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This approach could benefit several complex systems, including robotic platforms \cite{b36, b37 , autonomous vehicles \cite{b38 , and aircraft swarms \cite{b39 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we present two concrete applications to evaluate our method.
elements.sent-2411-036: The first application is based on simulating two-degrees of freedom for a robotic motion as it navigates across specified trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-037: For this, we extend on the work by Lutter et al. where they propose a deep neural network encoding Lagrangian Mechanics (DeLaN) to model the equations of motion for the robotic motion \cite{b48 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: We formulate their architecture in the context of 'knowledge blocks' and add residual layers to enhance the learning.
elements.sent-2411-039: For the second application, we test our approach on a more complex real-world problem where we model the behaviour of dynamic steering for autonomous grounded vehicles using a hybrid neural network architecture with the same concept of knowledge blocks.
elements.sent-2411-040: The well-established pure pursuit algorithm provides a physics-derived steering model accounting for dynamics like turn radius.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our architecture includes blocks of fully connected layers to discover intermediate unknown variables in the driving environment.
elements.sent-2411-042: It combines them with differentiable geometrical and kinematic operators from the pure pursuit model.
elements.sent-2411-043: We train this hybrid model observation data from an existing expert driving system inside a simulation environment.
elements.sent-2411-044: The goal is to learn the expert driver's behaviour or policy using supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-045: This forms a Behaviour Cloning problem, which involves transforming an expert’s demonstrations into i.i.d state-action pairs and learning to imitate the expert actions given each recorded state using a policy function.
elements.sent-2411-046: In the context of digital twins specifically, this approach is helpful since the underlying goal is to model the exact behaviour of a real-world system using observation data.
elements.sent-2411-047: This paper presents the following key contributions of our work:.
elements.sent-2411-048: It is the first (to our understanding) generic framework for embedding physics models into neural networks for end-to-end training on observation data.
elements.sent-2411-049: It is a significantly more interpretable hybrid neural network comprising learning blocks predicting human-understandable variables and physics blocks predicting the actual target variables.
elements.sent-2411-050: It allows the implicit discovery of unknown environmental variables that are not measured or observed.
elements.sent-2411-051: It outperforms the current state-of-the-art Physics Informed Machine Learning model for the Lagrangian-based robotic motion model in terms of accuracy of predicted torque and forces of motion.
elements.sent-2411-052: It outperforms conventional neural networks regarding key driving metrics on unseen road scenarios inside the simulation environment.
elements.sent-2411-053: It uses significantly fewer observation data, with fewer parameters, than conventional neural networks while outperforming key driving metrics inside the simulation environment.
elements.sent-2411-054: Physics Informed Machine Learning (PIML) is a recent approach that combines prior physics knowledge with conventional machine learning approaches to form hybrid learning algorithms of processes.
elements.sent-2411-055: Approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) encode analytical equations as loss terms to constrain deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-056: Making a learning algorithm physics-informed refers to applying appropriate observational, inductive, or learning biases that direct the learning process towards identifying physically consistent solutions \cite{b7 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Several methods have been proposed to incorporate observational bias in the form of input data generated from variable fidelity physical systems as a weak form of prior knowledge \cite{b7,b8, b9 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, abundant data must be available, especially for large deep-learning models, making scaling these approaches to other areas difficult.
elements.sent-2411-059: Another approach to PIML involves incorporating a learning bias into the learning model by penalizing the cost function of deep learning algorithms regarding adherence to a physical constraint, such as satisfying a partial differential equation (PDE) associated with the modelled process.
elements.sent-2411-060: In this way, the learning model attempts to fit both the observational data and the constraining equation or rule, for example, conservation of momentum and mass \cite{b10, b11, b12, b13, b14 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: This, however, only adds a soft constraint to the learning model as the physics no longer plays a direct, explicit role after the training of the model is complete.
elements.sent-2411-062: A more direct approach to PIML is incorporating an inductive bias into the learning model through crafted architectures of neural networks (NNs) to embed prior knowledge and constraints related to the given task, such as symmetry and translation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-063: For differential equations in particular, several approaches have been investigated to modify NN architectures to satisfy boundary conditions or encode a priori parts of PDE solutions \cite{b15,b16,b17,b18,b19 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, these models can struggle with extremely complex emergent behaviours arising from unknown dynamics outside the scope of hand-coded physics \cite{b44 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Differentiable physics-based PIML methods are based on incorporating inductive biases into the neural network architectures.
elements.sent-2411-066: This has allowed the integration of physical simulators directly into deep learning architectures through automatic differentiation \cite{b45, b46 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: But most of these applications have focused on modelling isolated phenomena rather than full-system behaviours and provide particular architectures suited to only the respective problem spaces \cite{b29, b30, b31 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: A generic framework for integrating physics models into learning-based data-driven models remains an open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-069: Furthermore, applying these methods in digital twin scenarios where the observational data and prior knowledge are limited and only partially known is restrictive and requires thorough exploration.
elements.sent-2411-070: The method defined in this paper herewith effectively addresses these issues.
elements.sent-2411-071: To address the problem of learning the behaviour of a target system with limited observation data and partially known physics, we propose a novel approach to integrate physics models into traditional neural networks to form modular architectures with `knowledge blocks'.
elements.sent-2411-072: These blocks are of two categories:.
elements.sent-2411-073: Physics blocks: representations of known physics equations as computational graphs comprising non-trainable parameters and static operators to reflect the mathematical operations of the equations.
elements.sent-2411-074: Learning blocks: conventional neural network layers comprising trainable parameters (weights).
elements.sent-2411-075: The physics blocks provide an a priori understanding of the system's fundamental behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-076: The learning blocks can learn unknown, intermediate variables in the input space required by the physics blocks to predict the target variable(s).
elements.sent-2411-077: Collectively, the connected blocks form a neural network that can be trained end to end on observation data generated by the target system.
elements.sent-2411-078: For the scope of this work, we assume that the physics block encoding the known physics equation(s) of the target system can be represented as a differentiable physics model.
elements.sent-2411-079: This is necessary to define the physics equation(s) as a computational graph that can be integrated with the conventional learnable layers of a neural network.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{pinn_general.png} \caption{Proposed Physics-Encoded Neural Network Architecture with Residual Blocks. The example shows a problem case with two target intermediate variables and two target values to be predicted. Intermediate, unknown variables $l_1$ and $l_2$ are predicted (discovered) by the learning blocks and passed to the physics block as inputs alongside the original input vector $X$. Intermediate feature vectors $V_1$ and $V_2$ are passed from the learning blocks to the residual blocks to predict residual target values $r_1$ and $r_2$. The residuals eventually add up with the physics-predicted target values $y_1$ and $y_2$ to output the final target values $a_1$ and $a_2$.} \label{fig:pinn_general}
elements.sent-2411-080: To explain the proposed architecture, we define a Behaviour Cloning problem where expert demonstrations from the target system are used to learn a policy to model observed states (inputs) to actions (target values) taken.
elements.sent-2411-081: For a set of expert demonstrations $T$ from the target system, we assume i.i.d state-action pairs as: \begin{equation}T = \{(x_1, a_1), (x_2, a_2) ... (x_n, a_n) \}\end{equation} We define a learning block as a set of fully connected layers $\mathcal{L (x;\theta)$ that takes observed state vector $x$ and outputs an intermediate state vector $l$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The learning block has a set of trainable weights $\phi$. \begin{equation}l = L(x;\phi)\end{equation} This intermediate state could include unknown variables from the environment or parts of the behaviour that are not directly defined by prior physics and thereby require empirical modelling.
elements.sent-2411-083: We also define our physics block as a computational graph $\mathcal{P}$ that predicts an action $a \in \mathcal{A}$, from the available set of actions $\mathcal{A}$ usually taken by the target system, using the observed state $x$ and the intermediate state $l$: \begin{equation}\hat{a} = P(x, l)\end{equation} We can then define our policy function $\pi$ to predict optimal action given the current state: \begin{equation}\hat{a} = \pi(x, L, P)  where  \hat{a} \in A\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: While the physics blocks provide key a priori knowledge to the model, they pose a significant problem in converging the loss function in the gradient descent algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-085: If the fixed relationships defined by these layers conflict with the learning blocks' modelled patterns, it can result in conflicting gradients.
elements.sent-2411-086: This can make the training process highly unstable, thereby affecting convergence.
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, static layers, such as those representing fixed physics equations, do not have learnable parameters or tweakable weights.
elements.sent-2411-088: When these static layers are integrated into a neural network, they can disrupt the flow of gradients to the learnable layers, often exacerbating the known problems of vanishing and exploding gradients.
elements.sent-2411-089: To overcome this problem, we propose to add `residual blocks' (similar to the concept introduced in the original ResNet paper \cite{resnet ) that branch out from the learning blocks to predict residual target value to be added to the physics block output.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This introduces alternative pathways for gradients to flow to the learning blocks, mitigating the effect of the static layers within the physics blocks.
elements.sent-2411-091: Figure \ref{fig:pinn_general shows an example case with two target intermediate variables and two target values to be predicted by the model.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Intermediate, unknown variables $l_1$ and $l_2$ are predicted (discovered) by the learning blocks and passed to the physics block as inputs alongside the original input vector $X$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Intermediate feature vectors $V_1$ and $V_2$ are passed from the learning blocks to the residual blocks to predict residual target values $r_1$ and $r_2$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The residuals eventually add up with the physics-predicted target values $y_1$ and $y_2$ to output the final target values $a_1$ and $a_2$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Consequently, we can update the following in our earlier example of a Behavioral Cloning problem.
elements.sent-2411-096: An intermediate layer output vector $V$ from the learning block $\mathcal{L}$ will be passed to a separate residual block $\mathcal{R}$, which also comprises trainable parameters $\phi_r$. \begin{equation}r = R(V;\phi_r)\end{equation} The residual target value $r$ will then be added to the output from the physics blocks to predict the action $\hat{a}$. \begin{equation}\hat{a} = P(x, l) + r\end{equation} Thereby, the final policy function to the Behaviour Cloning problem will become: \begin{equation}\hat{a} = \pi(x, L, P, R)  where  \hat{a} \in A\end{equation} The gradient descent algorithm optimises any suitable loss function $L(a, \hat{a )$ that defines the difference between $a$ and $\hat{a}$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Since the policy function is differentiable and comprises the affine connection of $\mathcal{L}$ and $\mathcal{P}$, the gradients from $\nabla{L}$ will flow through the chain of static operators from the physics block $\mathcal{P}$ to optimize the weights in the learning block $\mathcal{L}$, thereby modulating the learning of intermediate unknown variables using known physical constraints of the system.
elements.sent-2411-098: This essentially gives the final model the capacity to learn unknown, empirical relationships in data while maintaining the fundamental structure of the physics of the behaviour being modelled.
elements.sent-2411-099: We validate the effectiveness of our approach in two different experimental setups.
elements.sent-2411-100: The first involves modelling the dynamics of robotic motion using Lagrangian Mechanics (Euler-Lagrange equation) as the Physics Model in our physics-encoded architecture defined earlier.
elements.sent-2411-101: The second setup involves modelling steering dynamics for autonomous vehicle simulation.
elements.sent-2411-102: Here, we investigate the effectiveness of our approach in a more complex digital twin problem where prior physics only partially defines the system's dynamics.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Learning Lagrangian Mechanics for Robotic Motion Simulation
elements.sent-2411-103: We first investigate the effectiveness of our approach, especially the residual architecture's effect, on a Lagrangian mechanics problem.
elements.sent-2411-104: In this section, we propose to learn the dynamics of robotic motion using the Euler-Lagrange differential equation from Lagrangian mechanics as prior physics knowledge for a learning model.
elements.sent-2411-105: We build on the Deep Lagrangian Networks (DeLaN) by Lutter et al. \cite{b48 and extend their architecture in the context of our framework using knowledge blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-106: While the authors of DeLaN propose a framework very similar to ours, its applicability is restricted to Lagrangian Mechanics, not addressing the challenges associated with generalizability such as restrictions to gradient flow due to physics-based operators in the neural architecture.
elements.sent-2411-107: To address this, we propose to extend DeLaN architecture with a residual block and present the effectiveness of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-108: DeLaN incorporates non-conservative forces, generalized coordinates \(q\) and generalized velocities \(\dot{q}\) , which are the time derivatives of \(q\).
elements.sent-2411-109: Generalized coordinates \(q\) refer to coordinates which uniquely identify the configuration of the system being modelled.
elements.sent-2411-110: DeLaN defines the Lagrangian, \(L\) as a function of \(q\) and \(\dot{q}\), as: \begin{equation}L(q, \dot{q}) = T(q, \dot{q}) - V(q)\end{equation} where \(T\) is the kinetic energy and \(V\) is the potential energy of the system.
elements.sent-2411-111: For systems undergoing rigid body motion, the kinetic energy can be calculated as the \(T = \frac{1 {2}\dot{q ^\top H(q) \dot{q \), where \(H(q)\) is the mass matrix, which is symmetric and positive definite.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: The Euler-Lagrange equations of motion are given by: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial{L}}{\partial{\dot{q}}} - \frac{\partial{L}}{\partial{q}} = \tau\end{equation} Hence the Lagrangian equations of motion becomes: \begin{equation}\label{eq:euler_lagrange} H(q)\ddot{q} + \underbrace{ \dot{H}(q)\dot{q} - \frac{1}{2}( \frac{\partial}{\partial{q}} (\dot{q}^\top H(q)\dot{q}))^\top }_{c(q, \dot{q})} + g(q) = \tau\end{equation} Here, \(g(q) = dV/dq\) and \(c(q, \dot{q )\) describes the Centripetal and Coriolis forces.}
elements.sent-2411-113: DeLaN approximates the inverse model by representing the unknown functions \(g(q)\) and \(H(q) \) as feed-forward networks (or single network with two output heads).
elements.sent-2411-114: Rather than representing \(H(q)\) directly, the lower-triangular matrix \(L(q)\) is predicted and used to calculate \(H(q)=L(q)^\top L(q)\).
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, DeLaN approximates the inverse model using two learned variables \(g(q, \theta)\) and \(L(q, \theta)\) where \(\theta\) is the learnable set of weights for the feed-forward network.
elements.sent-2411-116: These variables are then fed to the operators in the Euler-Lagrange equation \ref{eq:euler_lagrange to predict the motor torque \(\hat{\tau}\).}
elements.sent-2411-117: Further details of the formulation of these equations and the neural network structure behind DeLaN can be found in the original paper \cite{b48 and therefore not expanded upon in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-118: We can reformulate the basic architecture behind DeLaN as a combination of knowledge blocks.
elements.sent-2411-119: Since the physics and learning blocks can be conceptually viewed as part of the original DeLaN architecture, we extend the architecture by adding a residual block.
elements.sent-2411-120: Figure \ref{fig:derlan illustrates the original architecture from \cite{b48 as a combination of learning, residual and physics blocks.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The feed-forward layers predicting \(g(q, \theta)\) and \(L(q, \theta)\) are illustrated as a learning block that output these intermediate variables into the physics block representing the Euler-Lagrange operators.
elements.sent-2411-122: In the figure, \(L\) is divided into off-diagonal (\(l_o\)) and diagonal (\(l_d\)) outputs from two different heads to guarantee positive definiteness.
elements.sent-2411-123: The theoretical understanding behind it can be found in the original paper \cite{b48 .}
elements.sent-2411-124: The residual block takes as input one of the intermediate layer outputs from the learning block and outputs the residual value $r$.
elements.sent-2411-125: This residual value is added to the motor torque \(\tau\) output from the physics block to form the final predicted motor torque value \(\hat{\tau _{target}\).}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{derlan.png} \caption{The computational graph from DeLaN \cite{b48} reformulated with learning, residual and physics blocks. The learning block (blue) comprises three separate heads to output $g$, $l_o$ and $l_d$. The physics block (orange) represents the mathematical operators from the Euler Lagrange equation. The residual block comprises a set of fully-connected layers, that use an intermediate layer output from the learning block as input. It predicts the residual value $r$ which is added to the predicted torque from the phyics block to output the final torque value $\hat{\tau}_{target}$} \label{fig:derlan}
elements.sent-2411-126: The complete computational graph forms our physics-encoded neural network that is trained end-to-end similar to the original DeLaN.
elements.sent-2411-127: The loss function is based on MSE (Mean Squared Error), minimizing the error between the observed ($\tau$ ) and predicted ($\hat{\tau}$) torque values.
elements.sent-2411-128: This is defined as: \begin{equation}L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{D}(\tau_i-\hat{\tau}_{i})^2\end{equation} We use the Mini-Batch Gradient Descent algorithm and Adam Optimizer to optimize the loss function, where $n$ is any arbitrary batch size for each sample in the gradient descent algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-129: The gradients for the learnable weights $\theta$ are calculated by the chain of function derivatives that involve the operators from the Euler-Lagrange equation, effectively guiding the learning to follow the physical constraints of the system.
elements.sent-2411-130: The residual blocks facilitate the learning process by providing alternate gradient pathways.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Steering Dynamics for Autonomous Vehicle Simulation
elements.sent-2411-131: We now apply this framework to a more complex digital twin problem where the available data and prior physics may not be substantially available to completely model the system dynamics independently.
elements.sent-2411-132: We investigate our approach in an autonomous driving task of steering a vehicle in a simulation as it manoeuvres around a race track.
elements.sent-2411-133: Using data generated from an existing driving agent within the simulation (treated as a reference agent), we train a feed-forward neural network architecture that combines fully connected layers that capture perceptual understanding with relevant mathematical operators from the physics-based steering model known as Pure Pursuit.
elements.sent-2411-134: Figure \ref{fig:pinn illustrates the proposed architecture and is explained in detail in section \ref{sec:pinn .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We use the TORCS open-source driving simulator as our experimental environment due to the tracks' diversity and rich non-visual sensory and perceptual information \cite{b3 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: The input parameter space offered to the model consists of a vehicle’s state variables and perception information from the surrounding environment.
elements.sent-2411-137: Table 1 summarizes the subset of the available variables from the simulation environment used as input parameters and actuators/actions for our model.
elements.sent-2411-138: The vector of 19 range finder sensors (\(R\)) comprises the distance between the track edge and the car centre within a range of 200 meters.
elements.sent-2411-139: Each value represents clockwise distances from 0 to 180 degrees to the car axis at 10-degree intervals.
elements.sent-2411-140: This is similar to how an automotive RADAR sensor is used to localize objects in the long-range vicinity of the autonomous vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-142: The same methodology defines the distances to surrounding traffic vehicles to define vector $O$, as Table \ref{tab:variables_parameters explains.}

The image from the TORCS simulation on the left shows a sample of sensors at different angles depicted with red arrows, which is further elaborated on in the figure on the right side. The maximum range of the sensors is 200m. A similar sensor setup defines distances to surrounding traffic vehicles ($O$).} \label{fig:radar_sensor}

elements.sent-2411-143: The pure pursuit algorithm is a path-tracking algorithm that steers a vehicle along a desired path, given a set of reference points to follow.
elements.sent-2411-144: The algorithm calculates the steering angle required to follow the path based on the vehicle’s current position and velocity \cite{b24 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: It operates based on the concept of "pursuit," where a hypothetical point, known as the look-ahead point, is established ahead of the vehicle on the desired path.
elements.sent-2411-146: The objective is to steer the vehicle towards this point, thereby ensuring path tracking.
elements.sent-2411-147: The algorithm computes the optimal steering angle required to reach the look-ahead point by analyzing the geometric relationship between the vehicle and the path.
elements.sent-2411-148: Firstly, the algorithm estimates the position of the look-ahead point by projecting it along the desired path based on the vehicle's current position.
elements.sent-2411-149: Subsequently, the algorithm computes the curvature of the path at the look-ahead point using mathematical techniques such as interpolation or curve fitting.
elements.sent-2411-150: Finally, utilizing the vehicle's kinematic model, the algorithm calculates the required steering angle to navigate towards the look-ahead point.
elements.sent-2411-151: The look-ahead distance is a control parameter in the context of the algorithm and affects the outcome in two contexts: 1) Regaining a path, i.e., the vehicle is at a “large” distance from the path and must attain the path.
elements.sent-2411-152: The effects of changing the parameter in the first problem are easy to imagine using the analogy to human driving.
elements.sent-2411-153: The longer look-ahead distances converge to the path more gradually and with less oscillation, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:lookahead .}
elements.sent-2411-154: It, therefore, depends on the state of the driving environment at any point in time to optimally set the look-ahead distance to manoeuvre the track safely.

elements.sent-2411-155: The Pure Pursuit model defines a reference point on the road as a vector of two observed scalar values: the heading difference (angle) to the direction of the reference point and the lookahead distance to the reference point.
elements.sent-2411-156: Formally, this can be represented as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:z} z =  l \theta_{target} \end{equation} where $z$ is the reference point, and $l$ and $\theta_{target}$ are the lookahead distance and heading difference accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-157: We assume that the vehicle follows the principle of a rigid body moving around a circle and, therefore, compute the required steering angle using this circle's instantaneous centre of rotation.
elements.sent-2411-159: Consequently, from the arc of rotation depicted in the figure, we can use the law of sine to calculate the curvature, \(k\), of the path to the selected point as: \begin{equation}\frac{l}{\sin{2\theta}} = \frac{r}{\sin(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta)} \frac{l}{\sin\theta \cos\theta} = \frac{r}{\cos\theta} \frac{l}{\sin\theta} = 2r\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:2} k=\frac{1}{r} = \frac{2\sin\theta}{l}\end{equation} Here, \(r\) is the radius of rotation for the vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-160: Furthermore, we know from the kinematic bicycle model that the radius of rotation has an inverse relationship with the steering vehicle as: \begin{equation}r = L / \tan(\delta)\end{equation} Here, \(\delta\) is the steering angle and \(L\) is the wheelbase of the vehicle (horizontal distance between rear and front wheel centers).
elements.sent-2411-161: Therefore, we can substitute \(r\) from the equation of curvature (Equation \ref{eq:2 ) to get the required steering angle as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pure_pursuit} \delta = \arctan(\frac{2L\sin\theta}{l})\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-162: Based on the approach introduced in section \ref{sec:approach , we formulate our physics block as the mathematical operations calculating the target steering angle within the Pure Pursuit algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-163: As previously discussed in section \ref{section:pure_pursuit , the selected reference point and look-ahead distance are key parameters that dictate the steering behaviour and are based on the geometry of the driving path ahead.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Consequently, we formulate our learning block as a set of fully connected layers to output the optimal look-ahead distance $l$ and heading difference $\theta_{target}$ to represent the selected reference point $z$ on the road to follow (see Equation \ref{eq:z ).}

These variables are passed as input to the physics block to predict the steering angle $\delta$. elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Two-Phased Behavior Cloning Using Physics-Encoded Neural Network} \label{algo:1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Demonstration Data 1 ($\mathcal{D}_1$), Demonstration Data 2 ($\mathcal{D}_2$), Loss Threshold ($\ell_{\text{thresh}}$) \State Output: Integrated Model ($M$) \State Initialize Integrated Model $M$ comprising three blocks $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{R}$, $\mathcal{P}$ \State Initialize learning block $\mathcal{L}$ (learnable) \State Initialize physics block $\mathcal{P}$ (non-learnable) \State Initialize residual block $\mathcal{R}$ (learnable) \State Initialize heuristic function $H$ for label generation \State \State \textbf{Phase I: Warm Start - Train Block $\mathcal{L}$ with Heuristic Labels} \Repeat \State Sample mini-batch of state-action pairs from $\mathcal{D}_1$ \State Generate labels using the heuristic function $H$ \State Forward pass through block $\mathcal{L}$ \State Compute loss ($\ell_{1}$) for predicted lookahead distance and target heading differences \State Backward pass and update the weights in block $\mathcal{L}$ \Until{convergence} \State \State Phase II: Train Complete Model $M$ \State Initialize block layers in $\mathcal{L}$ with weights from Phase I \Repeat \State Sample mini-batch of state-action pairs from $\mathcal{D}_2$ \State Forward pass through models $\mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{L}$ with static block $\mathcal{P}$ \State Compute loss ($\ell_2$) for predicted steering \State Backward pass and update weights in blocks $\mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{L}$ \Until{$\ell_2 < \ell_{\text{thresh}}$} \State \State \Return Trained model $M$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-185: We define a heuristic function to provide the learning block with proposed values of lookahead distance ($l$) and target heading difference ($\theta_{target}$) for each data point.
elements.sent-2411-186: The heuristic function selects the reference point to be in the direction of the furthest point on the road boundary from the vehicle, using the range-finding sensor vector \(R\) (refer to Table \ref{tab:variables_parameters ).}

elements.sent-2411-189: The sensors capture values every 10 degrees on this axis, clipped to a maximum value of 200m.
elements.sent-2411-190: These values depict the presence of a road boundary at the corresponding angle value.
elements.sent-2411-191: Formally, for \(R_t\) as the vector of range finder sensors from a data point $t$, we can define: \begin{equation}i = argmax(R_t)\end{equation} where $i$ is the index of the maximum distance value in vector $R_t$.

elements.sent-2411-194: The loss function is based on MAE (Mean Absolute Error), minimizing the error between the heuristic output and predicted values from the learning block.

elements.sent-2411-222: In this section, we present details on the experimentation and results on the problem of modelling steering dynamics in a simulated autonomous vehicle problem.
elements.sent-2411-223: We compare our Physics Encoded Residual Neural Network (PERNN) approach against two methods.
elements.sent-2411-224: The first is based on traditional fully connected neural networks (FCNN) to serve as a baseline for a purely data-driven method.
elements.sent-2411-225: The second method is based on physics-regularized neural networks which are currently one of the most popular generic physics-informed machine learning methods in literature.
elements.sent-2411-226: This is based on applying soft constraints to the learning process by appropriately penalizing the loss function of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-227: Assuming an external physics model \(P\), input variables \(x\), ground truth target variable \(y\) and any feed-forward neural network \(f\), the loss function of such a physics-informed network is: \begin{equation}L = L_{data} + L_{phy}\end{equation} where \(L_{data}\) can be any function measuring the similarity between the predicted variable \(f(x)\) and the target variable \(y\), while \(L_{phy}\) can be any function measuring the similarity between the predicted variable \(f(x)\) and the output from the physics model \(P(x)\).
elements.sent-2411-228: The loss function can also be represented as a weighted sum of \(L_{data}\) and \(L_{phy}\), where the weight value can be represented as a hyperparameter or learnable parameter.
elements.sent-2411-229: In the context of the experimentation in this section, we do not employ a weighted sum to calculate the loss term.
elements.sent-2411-230: Since it can be argued that the extra loss term \(L_{phy}\) ``informs" the neural network of the physical properties, we will refer to this approach as Physics Informed Neural Network (PINN) throughout the experimentation section ahead.
elements.sent-2411-231: The comparisons between the methods presented herewith are drawn in terms of regression metrics on the test dataset and driving performance inside the simulation on unseen tracks.
elements.sent-2411-232: We also analyse the unknown intermediate variables: lookahead distance and heading difference to reference points chosen by our trained PERNN-based agent in different driving scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-233: To generate data in TORCS as expert demonstrations for our experimentation, we use the implementation of an advanced heuristic-based agent provided by the simulation based on Ahura: A Heuristic-Based Racer for the Open Racing Car Simulator \cite{b47 .}
elements.sent-2411-234: The details of this implementation are irrelevant to the approach explained in this paper since, theoretically, this expert system can be any high-fidelity and accurate driving model.
elements.sent-2411-235: We generate state and actuator values at each time instance on each of the 36 race tracks in the simulation, corresponding to two driving scenarios: empty road and traffic bourne.
elements.sent-2411-236: We refer to these two collected datasets as $\mathcal{D _1$ and $\mathcal{D _2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: The race tracks are divided into three categories: road, dirt and oval speedway tracks based on variations in track geometry.
elements.sent-2411-238: Most of the oval speedway tracks offer relatively simple geometry compared to the more complex geometry on the road tracks in terms of sharpness and variation in the directions of turns.
elements.sent-2411-239: The dirt tracks offer significantly tougher terrain owing to sharper turns and high variation in the altitude values leading to dips and troughs, represented as the $z$ value in Table \ref{tab:variables_parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-240: Figure \ref{fig:track_geometry shows a sample of maps representing geometrical variations in each of the three track categories.}
elements.sent-2411-241: We reserve the data from a set of tracks as our test cases from both $\mathcal{D _1$ and $\mathcal{D _2$, where we analyze the behaviour of the trained models in the simulation.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: These include all dirt tracks and several road tracks.
elements.sent-2411-243: The goal is to test how well each approach performs in navigating new geometrical scenarios and their consequent capability for error recovery.

elements.sent-2411-244: We test four different agents in our experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-245: The first agent is based on the model trained using our Physics Encoded Residual Neural Network (PERNN) explained earlier in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-246: The second agent is based on the same framework as our method but without the residual block.
elements.sent-2411-247: This will help us illustrate the effectiveness of the residual-based architecture.
elements.sent-2411-248: We will refer to this model as Physics Encoded Neural Network (PENN).
elements.sent-2411-249: The third agent, as a benchmark, is based on a model trained using a conventional feed-forward neural network (FCNN) with a set of fully connected layers and ReLU activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-250: Finally, the fourth type of agent is trained using a physics-informed neural network (PINN) having the same base architecture as the FCNN.
elements.sent-2411-251: The output from all networks is a single steering value based on the state information provided to the model.
elements.sent-2411-252: For the PINN approach, the Pure Pursuit model serves as the physics model regularizing the loss function (discussed at the beginning of section \ref{sec:driving_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-253: The Pure Pursuit model takes as input appropriate values of lookahead distance ($l$) and target heading difference ($\theta_{target}$), and since there is no concept of learning blocks predicting intermediate variables in the PINN approach, we use our heuristic function from section \ref{sec:warm_start to generate these values for each data point in the training data.}
elements.sent-2411-254: This means that the regularizing term \(L_{phy}\) is calculated for empty road scenarios where the logic behind the heuristic function holds.
elements.sent-2411-255: For the traffic-based data points, \(L_{phy}\) is set to zero to not influence the loss value for traffic-based scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-258: We compare three different FCNN models with our PERNN model regarding mean absolute error (MAE) on the test dataset, average distance travelled, and average jerk in predicted steering on the test tracks during live simulation.
elements.sent-2411-259: The three FCNN models are trained on the collective data from $\mathcal{D _1$ and $\mathcal{D _2$, comprising a single phase of training, as opposed to the two-phased setup for the PERNN model (see section \ref{sec:training_method ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: The three FCNN models differ in architectural complexity and training data size (number of tracks comprised in training data) as shown in Table \ref{tab:model_evaluation_ext .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Since the size of the training data and the number of parameters in the model are related in terms of the bias-variance trade-off, each of the three FCNN models consumes a differing, dedicated size of training data for fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-262: We also compare the performance of the agents using two key driving metrics.
elements.sent-2411-263: The first metric is a macro-level statistic of the average distance travelled by the agents on the test tracks before they hit the road boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-264: The second, more micro-level metric is based on the smoothness factor of the driving trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-265: This is shown through the jerk and entropy values associated with the predicted steering angle during the test runs.
elements.sent-2411-266: Jerk is a third-order derivative of the predicted steering angle.
elements.sent-2411-267: If we refer to the predicted steering angle as $\delta$, then the steering velocity is calculated as $\delta^\prime$, steering acceleration as $\delta^{\prime\prime}$ and finally the jerk value as $\delta^{\prime\prime\prime}$.
elements.sent-2411-268: To keep experimental consistency, the PENN architecture for the learning and physics blocks is kept the same as that in the PERNN model.

elements.sent-2411-270: In summary, we investigate the effectiveness of the PERNN model against the FCNN and PINN counterparts based on the two areas:.

elements.sent-2411-319: This paper presents a novel physics-encoded neural network architecture for embedding physics models into neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-320: The concept of residual knowledge blocks allows this architecture to be used as a general framework for encoding physics knowledge into neural networks using any differentiable physics model.
elements.sent-2411-321: It is a promising research direction in terms of adding reliability and interpretability to neural network-based learning algorithms by providing the capability to discover unknown variables in the environment which are not part of the originally observed data.
elements.sent-2411-322: Furthermore, this method provides a direct and effective way to build prototype models rapidly for the general digital twin scenarios with limited data and physical definitions of real-world entities.
elements.sent-2411-323: We investigated our method in two distinct applications.
elements.sent-2411-324: The first involved modelling basic robotic motion using Euler-Lagrangian equations of motion as physics prior.
elements.sent-2411-325: We extended the current state-of-the-art method (DeLaN) to build our residual knowledge blocks and presented the effectiveness of the residual methodology in the context of our general physics-encoded neural network framework.
elements.sent-2411-326: The second application involved developing a steering model for autonomous vehicles in the TORCS simulation environment.
elements.sent-2411-327: We presented some key achievements here, including significantly improved generalizability of the driving model to new road scenarios compared to pure data-driven, fully-connected neural networks and the well-known physics informed (regularized) neural network method.
elements.sent-2411-328: This was achieved with much less model complexity and data requirements.
elements.sent-2411-329: We showed that this method outperformed both counterparts on unseen test track scenarios regarding driving metrics like average distance travelled before boundary hit and smoothness of steering trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-330: We presented an analysis of the effectiveness of our method to discover unknown variables such as the lookahead distance and the heading difference to the selected point on the road and how the model intelligently predicted these variables based on different road scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-331: This also presented valuable insight regarding the explainability of the model's decision-making based on what point on the road is selected to be followed for different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-332: It would be promising to test this approach further on a wide range of problems - specifically in the digital twin space where data availability is constrained and physics models are `ab-inito' or first principle.
elements.sent-2411-333: Further study on the behaviour of gradient flow through the network could help develop more robust residual blocks, especially for more complex physics where large physics blocks could present more constraints on the algorithm's convergence.. \backmatter.

elements.sent-2411-348: Physics Informed Machine Learning has emerged as a popular approach for modeling and simulation in digital twins, enabling the generation of accurate models of processes and behaviors in real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-349: However, existing methods either rely on simple loss regularizations that offer limited physics integration or employ highly specialized architectures that are difficult to generalize across diverse physical systems.
elements.sent-2411-350: This paper presents a generic approach based on a novel physics-encoded residual neural network (PERNN) architecture that seamlessly combines data-driven and physics-based analytical models to overcome these limitations.
elements.sent-2411-351: Our method integrates differentiable physics blocks—implementing mathematical operators from physics-based models—with feed-forward learning blocks, while intermediate residual blocks ensure stable gradient flow during training.
elements.sent-2411-352: Consequently, the model naturally adheres to the underlying physical principles even when prior physics knowledge is incomplete, thereby improving generalizability with low data requirements and reduced model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-353: The first is a steering model for autonomous vehicles in a simulation environment, and the second is a digital twin for climate modeling using an ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based model of Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) to enable gap-filling in flux tower data.
elements.sent-2411-354: In both cases, our method outperforms conventional neural network approaches as well as state-of-the-art Physics Informed Machine Learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-355: While physics-based models offer better accuracy and reliability of predictions, they are often computationally expensive and inflexible to be used in near-real-time simulations - especially in the context of Digital Twins \cite{Willcox2021, Wang2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-356: However, they popularly suffer in terms of reliability and interpretation.. \cite{Rasheed2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-357: The benefits of combining physics with data-driven models to target these limitations are found in several studies, especially in the context of developing simulation models in situations with limited availability of prior physics knowledge and real-world data \cite{Rasheed2020, Thelen2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-358: In the ubiquitous middle ground, some physics and data are known, with potentially missing parameters and initial/boundary conditions, and this is the most common scenario in models for digital twins in general.. \cite{Lu2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-359: An example is the recent work on new connections between neural network architectures and viscosity solutions to Hamilton–Jacobi PDEs \cite{Darbon2021 as shown in Figure \ref{fig:hj_pde_pinn .}}

elements.sent-2411-360: While existing work mainly focuses on isolated physical phenomena \cite{Li2019, Toussaint2018, SanchezGonzalez2018 , and lacks the generalization of the approaches to wider problems, we propose Physics Encoded Residual Neural Network (PERNN), a general framework to capture complex behaviour of real-world systems.}
elements.sent-2411-361: We also make our code available publicly on GitHub.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/saadzia10/Physics-Encoded-ResNet.git In this way, domain knowledge can inform the underlying neural network architecture while the gradient descent optimization using data can fill in gaps \cite{Seo2022, Stewart2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: The goal is to leverage interpretable mechanistic modelling to improve generalization and avoid relying solely on empirical correlations \cite{Greydanus2019, Raissi2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-363: This approach could benefit several complex systems, including robotic platforms \cite{Cranmer2020, Mrowca2018 , autonomous vehicles \cite{Ajay2018 , and aircraft swarms \cite{Mohan2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-364: The first application focuses on modeling dynamic steering for autonomous grounded vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-365: In this case, we develop a hybrid neural network architecture that integrates differentiable geometrical and kinematic operators from the well-established pure pursuit algorithm with learning blocks that discover intermediate unknown variables in the driving environment.
elements.sent-2411-366: The model is trained on observation data from an expert driving system in a simulation environment to learn the expert's behavior or policy using supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-367: This forms a Behaviour Cloning problem, which involves transforming expert demonstrations into i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-368: In the context of digital twins, this approach is particularly useful because it models the exact behavior of a real-world system using observational data.
elements.sent-2411-369: The second application demonstrates our approach in a real-world digital twin setting for climate modeling.
elements.sent-2411-370: Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE), a key metric representing the net CO$_2$ exchange between ecosystems and the atmosphere, is typically measured by flux towers that often suffer from data gaps due to instrumentation failures and other issues.
elements.sent-2411-371: To address this challenge, we model NEE using an ordinary differential equation (ODE) derived from an Arrhenius-type formulation of ecosystem respiration.
elements.sent-2411-372: Our Physics Encoded Residual Neural Network (PERNN) for NEE explicitly incorporates the NEE ODE into the network via a dedicated physics block, while learning and residual blocks predict critical intermediate variables such as temperature sensitivity (\(E_0\)), base respiration (\(r_{b\_night}\)), and the rate of change of ambient air temperature (\(\frac{dT {dt}\)).}
elements.sent-2411-373: This integration enables accurate gap-filling and forecasting of NEE, leading to improved digital twin fidelity for climate monitoring and forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-374: This paper presents the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-375: It is a significantly more interpretable hybrid neural network comprising learning blocks that predict human-understandable variables and physics blocks that predict the actual target variables.
elements.sent-2411-376: It outperforms conventional neural networks regarding key driving metrics on unseen road scenarios in simulation environments.
elements.sent-2411-377: It uses significantly fewer observation data, with fewer parameters, than conventional neural networks while outperforming key driving metrics.
elements.sent-2411-378: It achieves superior gap-filling performance in digital twin applications for climate modeling by accurately forecasting Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) from flux tower data, outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as Random Forest and XGBoost in key error metrics.
elements.sent-2411-379: It provides a robust digital twin framework for environmental monitoring by integrating an ODE-based physics model of ecosystem respiration directly into the neural network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-380: Making a learning algorithm physics-informed refers to applying appropriate observational, inductive, or learning biases that direct the learning process towards identifying physically consistent solutions \cite{Lu2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-381: Several methods have been proposed to incorporate observational bias in the form of input data generated from variable fidelity physical systems as a weak form of prior knowledge \cite{Lu2021,Kashefi2021, Li2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-382: In this way, the learning model attempts to fit both the observational data and the constraining equation or rule, for example, conservation of momentum and mass \cite{Raissi2019, Lagaris1998, Zhu2019, Geneva2020, Wu2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-383: For differential equations in particular, several approaches have been investigated to modify NN architectures to satisfy boundary conditions or encode a priori parts of PDE solutions \cite{Lagaris1998,Beidokhti2009,Wang2020,Cai2020,Darbon2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-384: However, these models can struggle with extremely complex emergent behaviours arising from unknown dynamics outside the scope of hand-coded physics \cite{Miles2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-385: This has allowed the integration of physical simulators directly into deep learning architectures through automatic differentiation \cite{Degrave2019, Holl2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-386: But most of these applications have focused on modelling isolated phenomena rather than full-system behaviours and provide particular architectures suited to only the respective problem spaces \cite{Li2019, Toussaint2018, SanchezGonzalez2018 .}
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/pinn_general.png} \caption{Proposed Physics-Encoded Neural Network Architecture with Residual Blocks. The example shows a problem case with two target intermediate variables and two target values to be predicted. Intermediate, unknown variables $l_1$ and $l_2$ are predicted (discovered) by the learning blocks and passed to the physics block as inputs alongside the original input vector $X$. Intermediate feature vectors $V_1$ and $V_2$ are passed from the learning blocks to the residual blocks to predict residual target values $r_1$ and $r_2$. The residuals eventually add up with the physics-predicted target values $y_1$ and $y_2$ to output the final target values $a_1$ and $a_2$.} \label{fig:pinn_general}
elements.sent-2411-387: To overcome this problem, we propose to add `residual blocks' (similar to the concept introduced in the original ResNet paper \cite{He2016 ) that branch out from the learning blocks to predict residual target value to be added to the physics block output.}
elements.sent-2411-388: We validate the effectiveness of our approach in two distinct experimental setups.
elements.sent-2411-389: The first study involves modeling steering dynamics for autonomous vehicle simulation.
elements.sent-2411-390: In this scenario, we investigate our method within a simulated digital twin problem where the available prior physics is relatively simple and only partially defines the system's dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-391: The second study addresses a more complex, real-world digital twin problem for modeling CO$_2$ emissions and gap-filling of Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) in flux data.
elements.sent-2411-392: Here, we examine the ability of our approach to integrate an ordinary differential equation that partially characterizes the behavior of emissions with data-driven learning of the remaining dynamics from flux measurements, thereby enabling a forecasting model for gap-filling in emissions data—an important application in the climate digital twin domain.

The intermediate layer output from the learning block is passed to the residual block to predict the residual value to be added to $\delta$ for the final steering value } \label{fig:pinn}
elements.sent-2411-397: We can then define the predicted steering value (target value) based on the policy function $\pi$ as: \begin{equation}\hat{\delta} = \pi(x, L, P)  where  \hat{a} \in A\end{equation} The loss function is based on Mean Squared Error (MSE), minimizing the error between the observed ($\delta$ ) and predicted ($\hat{\delta}$) steering angles.
elements.sent-2411-398: This is defined as: \begin{equation}L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\delta_i-\hat{\delta}_i)^2\end{equation} We use the Mini-Batch Gradient Descent algorithm and Adam Optimizer to optimize the loss function, where $n$ is any arbitrary batch size for each sample in the gradient descent algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-399: This allows the loss function to converge and optimize the weights in the residual and, more importantly, the learning block.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Case Study 2: Climate Digital Twins for CO$_2$ Emissions and Gap-Filling in Net Ecosystem Exchange Modelling
elements.sent-2411-401: The application of digital twin technology to climate systems has emerged as a promising avenue for enhanced monitoring and forecasting of atmospheric CO$_2$ dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-402: Digital twins, which provide a virtual representation of real-world processes, are particularly valuable in climate science where continuous, high-quality observational data are critical yet often incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-403: Flux towers continuously monitor key atmospheric scalars such as Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE), Latent Heat, and Sensible Heat \cite{zhuclementetal2022 using the eddy covariance (EC) method.}
elements.sent-2411-404: However, these measurements frequently exhibit gaps due to instrumentation failures, power shortages, or environmental interferences \cite{reichsteinetal2005, moffatet2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-405: In the context of a digital twin for climate applications, robust gap-filling methods are essential not only for data completion but also for accurately capturing the underlying physical processes governing NEE.
elements.sent-2411-406: Traditional gap-filling techniques—such as Non-Linear Regression, Look-Up Tables, and Marginal Distribution Sampling—have shown reasonable performance for short-duration gaps, yet they struggle with longer intervals of missing data \cite{moffatet2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-407: More recent approaches, such as the Random Forest Robust (RFR) method, have enhanced performance over longer gaps by improving the statistical robustness of the estimates, although challenges remain for nighttime measurements \cite{zhuclementetal2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-408: Integrating the underlying physics of NEE into the gap-filling process can address these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-409: As demonstrated in previous work, NEE dynamics can be modelled as a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) that decomposes the signal into a deterministic drift component—governed by temperature-dependent ecosystem respiration following an Arrhenius-type law \cite{lasslopetal2010, lloydtaylor1994 —and a stochastic diffusion term representing measurement noise, assumed to be Gaussian \cite{White2008686 .}}
elements.sent-2411-410: Embedding this SDE within a digital twin framework allows the inherent physics of ecosystem CO$_2$ exchange to directly inform the data reconstruction process, leading to more reliable and interpretable predictions.
elements.sent-2411-411: Our proposed Physics Encoded Neural Network (PERNN) framework naturally extends to this climate modelling use case.
elements.sent-2411-412: In this setting, the physics blocks encapsulate the SDE-based model of NEE, capturing both the deterministic Arrhenius-type response of ecosystem respiration and the inherent stochastic fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-413: Complementary learning blocks infer latent variables and compensate for unobserved dynamics during periods of data absence.
elements.sent-2411-414: Moreover, residual blocks not only aid gradient flow during training but also facilitate the integration of these physics-informed estimates with empirical corrections, ensuring that the digital twin maintains fidelity to both the established physical laws and the observational data.
elements.sent-2411-415: This hybrid architecture enhances gap-filling performance while simultaneously improving the interpretability of NEE trends, making it a robust tool for climate digital twins.
elements.sent-2411-416: By directly incorporating physical models into the neural network, our approach bridges the gap between purely data-driven methods and traditional physics-based modeling, thereby providing a scalable and generalizable framework for environmental monitoring and forecasting \cite{Tao2019, Jones2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-417: The observational data employed in this study were collected from a flux tower located in East Anglia, UK \cite{cummingdata2012022 from 2012 to 2019.}
elements.sent-2411-418: The tower is equipped with an eddy covariance (EC) system designed to measure key atmospheric scalars including Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE), sensible heat flux density (H), momentum flux (Tau), relative humidity (RH), vapor pressure deficit (VPD), global radiation ($\text{R _\text{g}$), friction velocity (Ustar), soil temperatures (Tsoil1 and Tsoil2), and air temperature at 2\,m ($\text{T _\text{air}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-419: The dataset spans eight years, from 2012 through the end of 2019, with measurements recorded at 30-minute intervals.
elements.sent-2411-420: These high-frequency measurements provide a rich temporal resolution of the ecosystem's CO$_2$ exchange dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-421: However, the dataset also presents common challenges such as missing values caused by power shortages, sensor malfunctions, or adverse weather conditions.
elements.sent-2411-422: These data gaps can range from short intervals to periods extending over several months, emphasizing the necessity for robust gap-filling methods that can reliably reconstruct missing measurements while preserving the underlying physical relationships.
elements.sent-2411-423: A detailed description of the measured variables is provided in Table~\ref{tab:climate_variables .}
elements.sent-2411-424: In this study, particular attention is given to the NEE variable, which is central to understanding the carbon dynamics of the ecosystem.
elements.sent-2411-425: Given that latent heat (L) is measured by the same instrument as NEE, any gaps in NEE often correspond to gaps in latent heat measurements; hence, latent heat is excluded from the analysis to maintain consistency.
elements.sent-2411-426: Additionally, time-based attributes such as season, hour, day of week, month, and day of year are incorporated to account for diurnal and seasonal variations.
